project_overview.project_development_objective: Provide access to electricity to households, institutions, and businesses in selected areas of Herat Province, Afghanistan
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 230,800 people (36,062 households) and 1,600 non-residential consumers (businesses, schools, health centers, mosques) in Herat Province, with potential expansion to 360,000 people by 2040

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is experiencing ongoing insurgency-related violence and fragile security situation; Herat Province is adjacent to Iran and Turkmenistan borders

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Electrification of Four Districts in Herat Province
key_components.description: Construction of 25km 110kV transmission line tapping from existing line, four 110/20kV substations (8/10MVA each), and medium/low voltage distribution networks to electrify households, businesses, schools, health centers and mosques in Chesht, Obe, Karokh and Pashtun-Zarghoon districts. Targets approximately 14,062 households (90,000 people) and 1,600 non-residential consumers.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Grid Densification, Extension, and Off-grid Pilots in Herat Province
key_components.description: Extension and intensification of grid to other parts of Herat Province; piloting of solar mini-grids and solar-hybrid mini-grids in villages unlikely to obtain grid electricity within 5 years; consideration of subprojects for IDPs and returnees. Targets approximately 22,000 additional households (140,800 people).

Support for project implementation, supervision, environmental and social safeguards, institutional strengthening of DABS, studies for grid extension and synchronization, grid code development, and financial modeling for DABS. Includes hiring of safeguards specialists and capacity building.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Da Afghanistan Breshna Sherkat (DABS) - the vertically integrated power utility of Afghanistan, with Ministry of Finance as borrower
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: DABS has established Project Implementation Unit (PIU) headed by Project Director reporting to Chief Financial Officer; Project Manager supported by implementation team with electrical engineers, procurement specialist, FM specialist, and safeguard specialists; coordination with Ministry of Energy and Water (MEW) for policy and strategy; third-party supervision agent for monitoring
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: International competitive bidding for major contracts (>USD 5 million); National competitive bidding for smaller contracts; Consultant Quality-based Selection (CQS) and Quality and Cost-based Selection (QCBS) for consulting services; Prior review by World Bank for high-risk contracts; Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system used for planning and tracking
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: DABS responsible for M&E; quarterly Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) within 45 days of quarter close; semi-annual internal audit; annual external audit by Supreme Audit Office; monitoring indicators tracked semi-annually including people provided with electricity service, transmission line constructed, substation capacity, grid studies, off-grid pilots, grievance resolution, CDC consultations, girls' schools electrified, and awareness campaigns

identified_risks.risk_description: High uncertainties in Afghanistan's operating environment; escalating insurgency-related violence; fragile political situation affecting government's ability to deliver services and programs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Technical assistance to help DABS engage local communities; strong government support from President and relevant agencies; community consultations and stakeholder engagement

identified_risks.risk_description: Deteriorating macroeconomic situation with slow growth since 2012 (1.3-1.5% annually); high unemployment (22.6%); poverty increasing (39% in 2014-15); may exacerbate fragility and affect last-mile connections and electricity use
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project fully funded by IDA Grant reducing direct exposure to macroeconomic volatility; contingencies allocated for Components 1 and 2; technical assistance for customer engagement; tariff adjustments to cover costs

identified_risks.risk_description: Absence of technical standards and grid code; unclear strategies for grid expansion and synchronization; challenges in financing infrastructure investment; DABS low initial capacity for adopting international utility practices
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component 3 will develop grid code, grid expansion and synchronization studies, and financial planning model; technical assistance for institutional strengthening; capacity building for DABS

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Volatile security situation affecting DABS, contractors, and World Bank ability to implement and supervise; potential difficulties securing contractors; delays due to limited movement and logistical problems; increased security-related costs; limited contractor and consultant availability
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project contingency fund (25% physical and price contingencies); third-party supervision agent (similar to ARTF model) conducting periodic on-site visits and monitoring; technical assistance for capacity building; advance procurement initiation to avoid delays

identified_risks.risk_description: DABS has limited fiduciary capacity and experience with World Bank policies; agreed FM arrangements under ARTF projects not yet fully implemented; challenging market conditions due to security concerns; history of procurement delays affecting project outcomes
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Hiring of additional FM staff (finance officer and cashier) within 3 months of effectiveness; preparation of FM manual for all World Bank projects; hiring of internal audit consultant; hiring of international procurement specialist; preparation of procurement processing manual; advance procurement initiation for Component 1; quarterly IFR submission; semi-annual internal audit; annual external audit by Supreme Audit Office

identified_risks.risk_description: Fragile situation in Afghanistan requires local-level support and acceptance; risk of community opposition or lack of engagement; need for regular consultation with affected people and beneficiaries
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Community consultations during project design and implementation; Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) to be established within 3 months of effectiveness; grievance redress committee with PAPs, local government, NGOs, CDCs, and DABS representatives; citizen engagement based on two-way dialogue; beneficiary satisfaction surveys; emphasis on consulting vulnerable groups and women; multiple channels for grievance submission

identified_risks.risk_description: Temporary and/or partial permanent land/asset loss during construction of transmission lines, substations, and distribution networks; potential impacts on heritage sites; labor influx risks; changes to drainage patterns; removal of vegetation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed; site-specific Environmental and Social Impact Assessments (ESIA) and Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMP) to be prepared before civil works; Resettlement Action Plans (RAP) where needed; screening for land disputes; recruitment of dedicated environmental, social, and health & safety specialists in Herat; Code of Conduct for workers; Chance Find Procedure for archaeological sites; climate and disaster risk screening completed

identified_risks.risk_description: Distribution line crossing wide river; potential construction and operational challenges in fragile security environment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of special distribution tower design (common practice); conventional investments using proven technology and internationally accepted technical criteria and standards; experienced supervision consultants

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level GRM to be established within 3 months of effectiveness; multi-level GRM with grievance redress committee consisting of PAPs, local government representatives, NGOs, CDCs, and DABS; multiple channels and locations for grievance submission; public awareness on GRM services; systematic tracking and resolution of complaints; target of 60% of grievances satisfactorily resolved within stipulated timeframe
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender analysis conducted; specific actions to address gender gaps; women included in safeguards consulting teams; targeted awareness campaigns for women on electricity uses and safety; emphasis on consulting women stakeholders; indicator for number of girls' schools with electricity service (target: 5); women mobilizers to conduct awareness campaigns
vulnerable_groups: Internally Displaced People (IDPs) and returnees (Component 2 consideration)
Project-affected people (PAPs) losing land or assets

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity in mind; benefits must accrue to local population to build community support; strong government support from President demonstrates commitment; community consultations and engagement throughout implementation; GRM to address community concerns; project provides direct benefits (electricity access) to local population; selection of routes screened for land disputes to avoid fueling conflicts; emphasis on local employment and transparent recruitment processes
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan is one of least developed countries (per capita GDP USD 634 in 2014; HDI rank 171/188); slow economic growth (1.3-1.5% since 2012); high unemployment (22.6%); poverty increasing (39% in 2014-15); large youth population (44% under 15 years); limited economic opportunities; gender disparities (female literacy 20%); rapid population growth; under-utilization of labor and capital resources; withdrawal of international forces (ISAF) created economic gap; ongoing insurgency-related violence; fragile political and governance environment
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Escalating insurgency-related violence increases fragility; volatile security situation affects project implementation, supervision, and contractor availability; security concerns limit movement and create logistical problems; increased demand for security-related resources; limited number of contractors and consultants willing to work in Afghanistan; potential for early termination or abandonment of assignments; third-party supervision agent used to supplement World Bank supervision due to security constraints; contingency funds allocated to address security-related cost increases; advance procurement to mitigate delays from security issues
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Component 2 specifically considers subprojects to provide electricity to Internally Displaced People (IDPs) and returnees; Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) triggered due to potential temporary and/or partial permanent land/asset loss during construction of transmission lines, substations, and distribution networks; Resettlement Action Plans (RAP) to be prepared where needed; payments to PAPs to be made before construction starts; selection of routes screened for land disputes
